I want my life with you to be unpredictable in the best possible ways. So that quiet moment, that surprise-less surprise, it’s exactly who I want to be with you. It’s exactly the best of who I am, and it’s exactly representative of the two of us: without fanfare or grandness, but true, caring, and honest.

I’m rationally and logically driven. Proposing to you on acid has been one of the most irrational and illogical things I’ve ever done. So congrats, I guess. You beat the system. You exert such an influence on me that you can make me act against my very nature.

If that isn’t love, then what it is?

entries.title: 10th Year
entries.note: I went public with this one. I still respect you deeply, even if you don't feel it as much these troublesome days.
entries.body:
10th Year Anniversary

I’m sure that meeting someone later in life can be a beautiful experience. You get to know who you are and what you want. You have experience on your side. You are a mature soul seeking out another of its kind.

Falling in love when you’re young can be so much more complicated. Forget knowing what you want from the other person, sometimes you don’t even know what you want from yourself. All of the self-doubt, all of the early second guessing of yourself and your partner… The process of self-discovery is so much messier when someone else is intimately involved. But as you share values and learn from each other, you start to align on the core of not only who you want to be, but also who you are.

And the simple truth is that, at this point, I don’t know who I am without Nicole.

That’s not even hyperbole. I don’t know what life would have had in store for me if I hadn’t met her, but I know that it would have been vastly different. The “me” that you all know would not exist without her. You know us as separate people, and of course we are, but in some small way, I also see us as an indivisible “us”. 

If we were to break up today, I would just curl up and die. It would rip me apart mentally. What would I do? Where would I go? What questions should I ask myself, when I’m no longer sure who I am? We’re in this together now, come what may. Through good times and bad times.

So here’s some news:

I’ve proposed to Nicole.

She said “sureee” (and then “yes", after clarifying that this wasn’t a joke).

I don’t have a picture of the ring to share because I didn’t buy one. I didn’t bring her out to dinner, ply her with flowers or gifts, and I certainly didn’t propose at the top of a mountain in a foreign country.

I didn’t do any grand romantic gesture - not because I don’t believe in romance, but because I believe the heart of romance lies in being true to one’s self and to one’s partner. Neither one of us are big-event people. When we sit in expensive restaurants, we feel like impostors. We playfully navigate our surroundings, arm-in-arm and heart-in-heart. The restaurant is just a backdrop - we’re there to enjoy the food, but really, we’re there to enjoy each other.

There is beauty in tradition. Holding a ceremony in the same way that your parents held a ceremony can bring warmth and joy to a nostalgic heart. A traditional proposal can be such a cathartic, powerful moment for people who’ve started every single thought of marriage within the context of a ring, or on one knee.

I grew up traditional. I always thought I’d propose with a ring. I always thought it’d happen on one knee. I always thought a lot of traditional things, simply because I’ve never had them challenged.
I’ve learned a lot of things with Nicole, but perhaps undoubtedly the most important thing I’ve learned with her is how and when to unlearn.

At the end of the day, my commitment doesn’t need to be ratified in the form of compressed carbon on a loop of processed rare earth. There is value in the symbol, but we’re going to choose our own symbol, one that makes sense to us.

We’ve been together for exactly 10 years now. We’ve been through some amazing experiences. We’ve explored the world together through travel, and each other’s minds through conversation, and both of these activities have been equally exciting. We’ve gone through tough times, nearly breaking up on several occasions (for complex reasons that have ended some real marriages). But we pulled through. At the end of the day, we communicated. And we haven’t (and don’t) always see eye to eye, but we respect each other deeply. 

I cannot think of another intelligence I would rather spend the rest of my life with. Not as a husband supported by his wife, but as equal partners in every single way.

entries.title: The Ascension of colour
entries.note: Oreos, 29 September 2019 at Dakota, written on 30 Sep / 1 Oct
entries.body:
We calmly awaited the ascension of colour.

The journey began slowly, surrounded by awkwardness, shrubbery, and stars. We watched the parade of idiocy and awkwardness pass by, and were moved to grin, to smile, and to laugh. But the world too, was moved.

It undulated before us, shifting, breathing, evaporating colours, dividing white into red, blue, and green, and below it all, an infinite web of violet. The clouds became splashes of slowly poured milk in the sky, and the city became an island of distant neon lights.

The ascension was complete. We were ready for beauty.

We chose to lose ourselves in art. We travelled through mirrored temples. Through circuses,  bars, ballrooms, burning streets, and high schools. Visions lifted us up. War broke me down.
We marveled at the duality of desire and repulsion. We marveled at everything.
Exhausted of artistic vision, we chose to marvel at each other instead.
Our minds devoured each other, although our bodies were slightly less up to the task at hand. So we changed task. We repurposed our motion from horizontal to vertical, and joined the world around us in movement. Eyes closed, naked, shameless, we expressed ourselves freely, carried by incessant dark rhythms that first enveloped us on the ocean floor.
We danced this way for hours until the world and sound around us stopped moving. It left us together, so very close together, until, at last, silence swallowed everything but the sound of our breaths at each others ears, our arms, bodies, and hearts and thoughts intertwined just a little tighter than was possible the night before.

entries.title: Commemorating GP
entries.note: Recorded after the fact — close to verbatim.
entries.body:
At the foot of the staircase, you pull open the black gate.

You start to ascend the stairs, one by one, as you always have. Six years of climbing and descending, a daily recursive ritual, never punctuated by the least bit of difference.

You are Grey Project’s gallery manager. Six years of familiarity with these dusty grey walls and this dimly-lit staircase. A wooden postbox that somehow still receives mail meant for previous tenants, now almost a decade gone. You are oddly familiar with this interim space, neither street nor gallery. A tunnel of quiet between origin and destination, the world outside and work. Today, your feet tread a little heavier. The gravity between steps increases almost imperceptibly, just enough for you to notice that by the last step, this trek took longer than usual. Maybe it was you, your subconscious mind slowing you down just long enough to capture this last ascent in richer detail. Maybe it was the space, extending invisible tendrils around you, as if trying to extend the time you spend in it. But you ascend nonetheless. You reach the apex of the stairs and wonder to yourself if this hyper-sensitivity to your surroundings will last all day. As if you had to treasure the next five hours beyond all others, marking the ending of something of great importance.

You haven’t even stepped into the gallery yet.
 So you enter. Close the door behind you.
 The corridor waits.
 Hours pass, although they seem to alternate between taking minutes and years. The door opens, then closes behind you as you step out.
 The corridor waits.

You are no longer Grey Project’s gallery manager. Six years of familiarity with these dusty grey walls and this dimly lit staircase, but they are no longer yours. You entered a host but you leave a guest. You place your foot on the top step. You’ve never done this before. You’ve never left Grey Projects as an observer. Is this what visitors feel when they leave after a show? You take another step. The air is light but heavy. You are happy but sad. Most of all you are a little lost. You know you need to take the next step, both figuratively and literally, but both of these tasks seem almost alien to you.

What does it feel like to break-up with a worthy cause?
 What does it feel like to say goodbye to a relationship that has run its course?
 You realise that it feels like life. It feels like a path walked. It feels like goodbye, but more like the French would say it. Au Revoir. Until I see you again. You have overseen a multitude of efforts over the years, but you’re only now realising that you too have been a work-in-progress, refined by your responsibilities and efforts and sleepless nights. You too have been forged and tempered. You are a product of your history at Grey Projects, and no matter what happens next, always will be.

You continue to descend the stairs, one by one, as you always have. Six years of climbing and descending, a daily recursive ritual,
finally punctuated with a little, but important, difference.

entries.title: 9th year
entries.note: remember that time I wore a suit to that Russian restaurant wtf so cringe
entries.body:
Happy 9th Anniversary

It’s honestly very difficult to write something creative and mind-blowing every year. As the years grow, so does the importance of the date.

From “it’s been a year!” to “wow, we’ve been together for five years”, to “can you believe we’ve been together for 9??” The system compels me to impress. As the numbers add up, so should the meaningfulness of the letter… or at least, that’s what tradition dictates.

But we’ve never been a traditional couple.

You’ve asked a lot from me over the years. Always more. A better listener. A more patient partner. Always qualities that I should want for myself. But you’ve never asked more for yourself (except that one semi-reasonable but infuriating thing, you know what it is).

You don’t need escalation. You value our shared meals of $3 chicken rice just as much today as you did when we first hung out. We don’t need flowers, chocolates, or anything else society tells us we should want for each other. We don’t need fancy dinners or other ways for our wallets to serve as proxies for our hearts. No, I love feeling like an outsider with you. I love how we dress up in fancier clothes for special occasions, but always feel like we’re just playing at luxury because we don’t belong there. Because we don’t need to belong there. We don’t need “the life” because we have our life. Our little, simple, complicated, amazing, 9-year-in-the-making life.

So this letter isn’t longer or grander than the others. It isn’t even more poetic or necessarily more meaningful. But it’s us today.

Little,
simple,
complicated,
and amazing.

Just us.

entries.title: 8th Year
entries.body:
I was a boy, at first.
A boy who grew up under conservative christian parents. A boy protected from the world, but with an insatiable appetite to interact with it.

You were a girl, at first.
A girl whose rebellion won her a lip piercing at a young age. A girl who partied and broke into haunted hospitals and lived life to the fullest.

I was in love, at first.
I was in love with your hair, with your eyes, with your clothes. Then with your charm, with your spirit, with your words. Then with your self, with your all, with your soul.

That process took a few months. This relationship took a few years. I’m no longer a boy to you, and you’re no longer a girl to me. At some point, I, self, us, you, we, it all became confusing.

I lost the thread of where I ended and where you began. A weave of neurons, clusters of memories attached to each other by synapses, firing thousands of memories into each other each second. Every location holds your name- you are this room. You are Tampines, Bedok, Tanah Merah, and Starbucks. You are Berlin, Gengenbach, Budapest, and Prague. You are the Californian coast and the Redwood forest. You are foxes. You are dogs. You are cows. You are cats.

Then.
That is when I understood.
You were everything to me.

Happy 8th year together, love.

entries.title: 7th Year
entries.body:
Honestly? I hate so much about you. I hate traveling with you, I hate how you always win in our arguments, I hate how you’re so creative, I hate the way you talk to me, and I hate our quiet moments. The way we look in public together is just ridiculous. I hate how I can barely recognize who I am now compared to who I was before I met you. I basically can’t stand it when you bring up old shit we’ve been through, and it really pisses me off when you start asking me a ton of questions right before I go to sleep.

Sometimes, I want you to break up with me.

Happy 7th year.

Honestly? I love so much about you.

I hate traveling with you.
I basically can’t go anywhere in Singapore without reminiscing on where we’ve been and what we’ve done (including things we shouldn’t have done in places we shouldn’t have been). But I hate traveling without you even more. I could have breakfast in a place where I’m told you can almost touch the sun, take a trip towards the setting sun only to find that its resting place lies in an endless sea of fireflies, or sleep in a room built over the intersection of two rivers, cushioned by nature’s zephyrs… but feel no desire to visit any of these things. Not anymore. Not without you.

I hate how you always win our arguments.
It reminds me that as stubborn and as intelligent as I think I am, I am often wrong. It reminds me that I still have a lot of room to grow, and issues to overcome. It teaches me that logic is not a language of emotion, and that being right does not always mean that I have won.

I hate how you’re so creative.
I’m so jealous of your midas touch. I am a writer. I write. You are creative. You draw, paint, sculpt, imagine, plan, build. I am creation in thought. You are creation in action.

I hate the way you talk to me.
You know exactly what to say to me to defuse me. You know exactly how to take my funny jokes and make them just a bit funnier (what you call “actually funny”). You know how to make me laugh, and as evidenced by that night not too long ago, exactly how to make me completely break down and cry (in a good way). You talk to me like you know me. Maybe you do.

I hate our quiet moments.
As a writer, I hate how much we’re able to express to each other without the use of a single word. I hate our little naps, when we lie quietly by each other until we fall asleep. Each experience is a novel, written in the air, that I have never been able to capture in ink.

The way we look in public is just ridiculous.
You’d think we just got together. You’d think we were a newly attached, wide-eyed, honeymoon-period, over-the-top must-continue-touching-you kind of couple. How we basically haven’t taken an escalator in 7 years without hugging each other. How we haven’t gone a single day without a kiss, or two, or many many more.

I hate how I can barely recognize who I am now compared to who I was before I met you.
I don’t want to forget who I was. It’s an important part of me, one that I can look back on and feel so grateful to for having gotten with you. One that I can see changing, bit by bit, year by year. Becoming a less rigid person, one able to express himself more freely, to let go, to enjoy life and even bend the rules a little (sometimes).

I basically can’t stand it when you bring up old shit we’ve been through.
You just go on and on about our times together. In the US, in Singapore, in Japan, in Europe, in your house, in my hostel, at LASALLE… I love it. Until you tell me for the 50th time that you think about so-and-so everyday.

It really pisses me off when you start asking me a ton of questions right before I go to sleep.
This one, yeah, it really does. What? No one’s perfect.

Sometimes, I want you to break up with me.
I want to remember what it would be like to be without you. To say goodbye to everything that we’ve become. Everything that we’ve been, everything that we’ve done. To say goodbye to all the words we’ve invented, all the things we’ve created, all the memories we’ve imprinted. Everything. I want to realize how much of me is really “us”. I want to be devastated, just so I remember how much I love you.

It’s been seven years. Seven turbulent, long, but incredible years. And here we are.

Happy 7th Anniversary, love.

entries.title: 4th Year
entries.body:
Bittersweet 4th Anniversary.

As a child, I spent a lot of time in front of a computer screen.
A lot of light traversed the chasm between that monitor and me.
In the dark, you could look at the patterns of that light on my face, and tell that I was drawing.
You could see a glimmer in my eyes, as I exercised my imagination. See the streaks of red, blue, and green, as I crudely created "art" with my mouse.
You would have seen me smile a lot. You would have thought that I had found happiness in a computer screen. You would have been right, but you would have been wrong.

As a teenager, I spent a lot of time in front of a computer screen.
The light that is reflected on my face is far more colourful now. Bright flashes, sudden changes in illumination, streaks of color. A brow, often furrowed in concentration. Sudden bouts of soft swearing and elated grins. You would guess: computer games.
You would have seen me smile a lot. You would have thought that I had found happiness in a computer screen. You would have been right, but you would have been wrong.

As a college student, I spent a lot of time in front of a computer screen.
The light has dimmed. The games are still there, but their light is often muted, tempered, balanced with the dull white glow of a text document being written. Thousands of little black lines being created across the screen. So much focus, so much hard work. So many hours that sleep will never be able to claim back. But that look, that satisfaction when that little white window is full of black lines. That sense of accomplishment.
You would have seen me smile a lot. You would have thought that I had found happiness in a computer screen. You would have been right, but you would have been wrong.

And now, as a young adult, I spend a lot of time in front of a computer screen.
The light is very dim. The games are still there, the work is still there, but there's something else. It's dark now, a lot of the time. A slight reddish tinge, reflected on my face. You will see me shake my head, sigh in frustration, roll my eyes. You will see me cry. The light coming from the monitor is always moving, and the expression on my face with it.
You would have seen me frustrated a lot. You would have thought that I had found unhappiness in a computer screen. You would have been right, but you would have been wrong.

You would have been wrong, because you wouldn't have understood, just from looking at my face, that as I shook my head, it's because I couldn't believe how silly you looked on Skype at that moment. You would have been wrong, because you wouldn't have known that I sighed in frustration from the terrible joke you just made, yet thought you were so clever for making it. You would have been wrong, because you wouldn't have guessed that I had rolled my eyes at your refusal to go to sleep, even though it was already 5 am. You would have been wrong, because you couldn't ever have known that those tears came from watching an unbearably cute cat video that you shared with me.

You would have been wrong, but you would have been right.

You would have been right, because I had found a sort of unhappiness in a computer screen.
You would have been right, because I can't reach out to you and touch your face. You would have been right, because I can't hug you when you need me to. You would have been right, because seeing you on a screen is amazing, but it's not enough.

You would have been right, because today, on our 4th Anniversary of being together, I can't even look into your real eyes.

You are away from me for now, and this is wrong. But I believe, I know. We will make this right.

entries.title: What I will miss about my kitty
entries.note: Seems we thought about this way back when
entries.body:
What I will miss about my kitty.

Hey meow meow,

So, I expect this will be a bit of a sobering experience, but a good one. It's true that maybe I don't spend enough time thinking about the good things that we have going. But I do, and I realize what an amazing relationship we have, even if it goes bad once in a while. So as you requested, here are a few of the things that I treasure most about our relationship, and what I will most definitely miss about you if we ever break up.

-Never a dull moment: I've never been bored with you, ever. It may not always be good news, but there's always news with you. I haven't lived the most exciting social life since I graduated from Lasalle, and in a way, I kind of live socially through you. You have that kind of social life that I never had, and in a way I get to experience what it's like being friends with the kind of people that I might not want to get close to. But now that I am closer to them, it's quite an experience, and has allowed me to change how I see things and people. I'm super grateful for that, and I don't know that I'll be able to have it again if we don't make it, so I will miss that.
-Loyalty: You're really the most loyal person I know. You've made your share of mistakes, and even did some dumb things after getting with me (like showing John your underwear at Zirca), but I never doubted that your intent was anything close to disloyal. I'm not saying that I don't believe you've ever had doubts, especially when you were feeling down, but through it all, I know that you would never, ever cheat on me, and I'm glad that I was able to find someone who feels just as strongly as me about this core issue.
-Honesty: You are so crazy honest (with me) that it sometimes comes across as a bad thing. Now don't get me wrong, I am also incredibly honest, but sometimes the way you approach things without sugarcoating them at all can be a bit abrasive. On the one hand, it can be tiresome and troubling, yes, but on the other... I know that I can trust you, and that you'll tell me everything that I want to hear, and everything that I don't, but need to. I know a lot of people, myself included, that would rather keep certain things to themselves just to avoid the trouble of having to deal with them, but you're not one of those people. I can really appreciate that. I'm not saying that it's the best approach however... a little balance can be good- but I know that if it's something that matters, even if it puts you in a bad light, you'll tell me.
-Our kittyness: Obviously I'm going to miss everything that makes us kitties. I'm not stupid, and I know that it is sometimes just a shield that we put up (or at least, that I put up) to help me cope with things, to give myself a bit more time. It's easy not to talk about serious issues when the only thing that's happening in coversation is noms and cuddles and wuggles. But the kittyness is -not- just a shield. It's really a huge part of who were are together, part of our shared identity, and I love every last bit of it with every piece of my heart. I will never, ever forget our kittyness, what it means to us, and how much each huggle that we had means to me.
-Your face (and body): I've said this a million times, and alluded to this issue in probably every single serious letter I've written to you, and probably will continue to do so in the future- I find you attractive (sexy attractive, not just cute attractive), and I will miss your looks if we ever break up. You are doubtlessly the most attractive girlfriend that I've ever had, and I will miss that.
-Your artistic ability: I'm really super impressed with what you can do, aesthetically. You touch photoshop, it's gold. You touch paper clay for nearly the first time, you create an amazing looking wand. I have no doubt that anything you put your mind to doing artistically, you will do amazingly. One of the reasons that I push you to do more graphic design work is because I know that you can do truly great things there, and that I just want to see more of your work. A lot of times when I'm walking around and see something artistic, I think to myself: "Nicole could do better if she wanted to try".
-Your dedication to me: I DON'T take it for granted. I really appreciate just how much you care for me, and I'm impressed by the number of chances that you give me on issues in which I clearly fall short, again and again.

entries.title: 3rd Year
entries.body:
Hey Kitty Monster,

Amazing. It's been 3 years. Well, almost. I'm cheating by typing this out first, but I doubt that you'd want to spend the first 10 minutes of our 3rd-year-day together watching the Skype window go "Red Lebrun is typing…"

I'm not sure where to go with this letter. Should I recap some of my most amazing experiences with you, such as your two trips to the U.S to meet me? Should I elaborate on how I've finally become a braver person, able to stand up to my parents and for you? Should I somewhere mention the breakthrough that I've had on the religious/marriage front? There are so many ways to remember and think about those things, so maybe I'll take another direction and just talk about something else that I don't often, about our love and where this relationship is going.

These have been, doubtlessly, some of the most amazing years of my life. Probably the best three.
I'll admit I had some incredible struggles during this same period… But although we sometimes rocked the boat a little too hard, we're still here, strong, in love, and happy together. I don't know if I would have predicted that we'd make it to three years together, but I am incredibly happy that we put in the extra effort to go the extra mile and stayed together, even with long distance getting in the way.

This might sound really stupid, but although I've had dark thoughts about our future before, I've also recently had very optimistic thoughts about us, about a long term future for us. A future that I believe we can really make work if we both want and try to. I won't get ahead of myself of course, but it's encouraging, isn't it?

Anyway, here's to three years together, and with a bit of kitty magic, to our fourth.

With love, and kittily yours,
Your Sex Kitty.
